theorem:
\label{thm:lower}
Fix an integer $k\ge 3$. Then, as $N\to\infty$,
\[
f_k(N)\ge (\log N)^{c_k-o(1)},
\qquad
c_k:=\frac{k-2}{e ((k-2)!)^{1/(k-2)}}.
\],
sketch: To prove the main theorem, they use a ``prime bucketing'' construction: partition primes in a suitable interval into many disjoint blocks of comparable harmonic sum, and form squarefree integers by choosing exactly $k-2$ primes from each block. A short combinatorial lemma then forces LCM-$k$-freeness, while the harmonic sum factorizes and can be optimized over a free parameter.,

mcq.question:
Fix an integer $k\ge 3$. For $N\ge 1$, write $[N]:=\{1,2,\dots,N\}$, and define
\[
f_k(N):=\max\left\{\sum_{a\in A}\frac1a:\ A\subseteq [N]\ \text{is LCM-$k$-free}\right\},
\]
where $A$ is called LCM-$k$-free if there do not exist distinct $a_1,\dots,a_k\in A$ such that all pairwise least common multiples are equal, i.e.
\[
\operatorname{lcm}(a_i,a_j)=\operatorname{lcm}(a_1,a_2)\qquad(1\le i<j\le k).
\]
Define
\[
c_k:=\frac{k-2}{e\,((k-2)!)^{1/(k-2)}}.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic lower bound for $f_k(N)$ as $N\to\infty$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
\[
f_k(N)\ge (\log N)^{c_k+o(1)}\qquad (N\to\infty),
\]
with $c_k=\dfrac{k-2}{e\,((k-2)!)^{1/(k-2)}}$.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
\[
f_k(N)\ge (\log N)^{c_k-o(1)}\qquad (N\to\infty),
\]
for some positive constant $c_k$ depending only on $k$.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
\[
f_k(N)\ge (\log N)^{c_k-o(1)}
\]
for every sufficiently large $N$, where now $c_k=\dfrac{k-2}{((k-2)!)^{1/(k-2)}}$.

mcq.question:
Let $K$ be a number field, let $M,N\ge 1$ be integers such that the modular curve $X_1(M,MN)$ has genus zero, and let $p$ be a prime divisor of $MN$. For each $B$, let $\mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B;K)$ denote the set of elliptic curves $E/K$ of naive height $\mathrm{Ht}(E)\le B$ for which there exist a point $P\in E[MN](K)$ and a $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline K/K)$-stable cyclic subgroup $C\le E[M](\overline K)$ of order $M$ such that
\[
E[M](\overline K)=\langle NP\rangle\oplus C,
\]
so that $(E,P,C)$ defines a $K$-point of $\mathcal Y_1(M,MN)$. Let $\Sel_p(E)$ be the $p$-Selmer group of $E$. For $m>1$, let $\mathcal G_m(K)\subseteq (\mathbb Z/m\mathbb Z)^\times$ be the subgroup of residue classes $a$ for which there exist infinitely many prime ideals $\mathfrak p\subset \mathcal O_K$ with norm $N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\mathfrak p)=q$ a rational prime satisfying $q\equiv a\pmod m$. Here $A(B)\gg (\log B)^\theta$ means a lower bound by a positive constant multiple of $(\log B)^\theta$ in the usual asymptotic sense. If, in addition, the quadruple $(M,N,p,\mathcal G_{MN}(K))$ is not one of
\[
\begin{gathered}
(1,5,5,({\mathbb Z}/5{\mathbb Z})^\times),\quad (1,7,7,\{1,4,7\}\text{ or }({\mathbb Z}/7{\mathbb Z})^\times),\\
(1,10,5,({\mathbb Z}/10{\mathbb Z})^\times),\quad (2,2,2,\ast),\quad (2,6,3,({\mathbb Z}/6{\mathbb Z})^\times),\\
(3,3,3,\ast),\quad (3,6,3,\ast),\quad (4,4,2,({\mathbb Z}/4{\mathbb Z})^\times),\quad (5,5,5,\ast),
\end{gathered}
\]
what is the strongest statement that can be proved about
\[
\frac{1}{\# \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B; K)} \sum_{E\in \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B;K)} \# \Sel_p(E)
\]
as $B\to\infty$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists an explicit constant $\theta>0$, depending only on $M,N,K,$ and $p$, such that
\[
\liminf_{B\to\infty}(\log B)^{-\theta}\,\frac{1}{\# \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B; K)} \sum_{E\in \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B;K)} \# \Sel_p(E)>0.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists an explicit constant $\theta>0$, depending only on $M,N,K,$ and $p$, and a sequence $B_j\to\infty$ such that
\[
\frac{1}{\# \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B_j; K)} \sum_{E\in \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B_j;K)} \# \Sel_p(E) \gg (\log B_j)^\theta.
\]
In particular, the displayed averages are unbounded.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exist explicit constants $\theta>0$ and $c>0$, depending only on $M,N,K,$ and $p$, such that
\[
\lim_{B\to\infty}(\log B)^{-\theta}\,\frac{1}{\# \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B; K)} \sum_{E\in \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B;K)} \# \Sel_p(E)=c.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists an explicit constant $\theta>0$, depending only on $M,N,K,p,$ and $\mathcal G_{MN}(K)$, such that
\[
\limsup_{B\to\infty} \frac{1}{\# \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B; K)} \sum_{E\in \mathcal E_{(M,MN)}(B;K)} \# \Sel_p(E) \gg (\log B)^\theta,
\]
and the same conclusion still holds even without excluding any of the listed exceptional quadruples.

theorem:
For almost all irrational $\theta\in [0,1]$, if we denote by $q_k/p_k$ the sequence of convergents of the continued fraction expansion of $\theta$, then one has
$$\lim_{k\to \infty}\frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}=\frac{16}{\pi^3}\sum_{n\ge 1 \textrm{ odd}}\frac{1}{n^3\sin(n\pi\theta)}.$$

mcq.question:
Let $\theta\in[0,1]$ be an irrational number, and let $q_k/p_k$ denote the $k$-th convergent in the continued fraction expansion of $\theta$. Here, for a rational number $q/p\in(0,1)$, define
$$\sigma_2\!\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)=\sum_{(j,k,\ell)\in \Delta_p} (-1)^{\lfloor jq/p\rfloor+\lfloor kq/p\rfloor+\lfloor \ell q/p\rfloor},$$
where
$$\Delta_p=\left\{(j,k,\ell)\in\mathbb Z^3:\; 0<j,k,\ell<p,\; j<k+\ell,\; k<j+\ell,\; \ell<j+k,\; j+k+\ell<2p,\; j+k+\ell\text{ odd}\right\}.$$ What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the limit of the normalized quantity $\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)/p_k^2$ as $k\to\infty$ for Lebesgue-almost every such irrational $\theta$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: $\displaystyle \lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}=\frac{16}{\pi^3}\sum_{n\ge 1}\frac{1}{n^3\sin(n\pi\theta)}.$

choices.label: C
choices.text: $\displaystyle \frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}$ converges to a finite real limit for Lebesgue-almost every irrational $\theta\in[0,1]$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: $\displaystyle \lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}=\frac{16}{\pi^3}\sum_{n\ge 1\ \mathrm{odd}}\frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n^3\sin(n\pi\theta)}.$

choices.label: E
choices.text: $\displaystyle \lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}=\frac{16}{\pi^3}\sum_{n\ge 1\ \mathrm{odd}}\frac{1}{n^3\sin(n\pi\theta)}\quad\text{for every irrational }\theta\in[0,1].$

mcq.question: Let \(\mathbb A\in\{\mathbb H,\mathbb O\}\), viewed as a real Euclidean space with its standard norm \(\|\cdot\|\), and let \(P_0(x)\in\mathbb R[x]\) be a central polynomial over \(\mathbb A\). Suppose its zero set \(V_0=Z(P_0)\) contains a non-real manifold of dimension \(d_{\rm M}>0\). Let \(P_\epsilon(x)\in\mathbb A[x]\) be a generically non-central analytic deformation of \(P_0\), meaning that the coefficients depend analytically on \(\epsilon\in\mathbb R\), \(P_\epsilon(x)=P_0(x)\) when \(\epsilon=0\), and for \(\epsilon\neq 0\) near \(0\) the automorphism stabilizer \(G_{P_\epsilon}=\{g\in\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb A): g(a_k)=a_k\text{ for all coefficients }a_k\}\) is trivial. Let \(V_\epsilon=Z(P_\epsilon)\). Define \(\mathcal{V}_\epsilon(x)=\|P_\epsilon(x)\|^2\) and consider the gradient flow \(\dot{x}=-\nabla \mathcal{V}_\epsilon(x)\). As \(\epsilon\to 0\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the collapse under this flow?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and the collapse timescale exhibits critical slowing down with \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and for a generic non-central analytic deformation the characteristic collapse time scales as \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-1}\), reflecting a restoring rate that is linear in \(\epsilon\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and the characteristic collapse time diverges as \(\epsilon\to 0\), without asserting the exact power law.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and analyticity of the deformation already forces the Lojasiewicz exponent to be \(1/2\), so \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\) for every analytic deformation, including non-generic ones.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The gradient flow drives trajectories into the attractor set \(V_\epsilon\) with critical slowing down \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\), but in general this gives only a retract of a neighborhood of \(V_0\), not a deformation retract of \(V_0\) onto \(V_\epsilon\).

theorem: \label{thm:main} An extreme ray $r$ of the SONC cone on a finite ground set $\mathcal{A}\subseteq\NN^{n}$ is not exposed if and only if $r=\R_+ \mathbf{x}^\alpb$ for some $\alpb\in \mathcal{A}$, and there exists a circuit $(S,\betab)$ on $\mathcal{A}$ such that $\alpb \in S$ and $\betab\neq \alpb$ is even.,
sketch: The result is proved by (i) "showing separately that the extreme rays that correspond to the conditions in the theorem are not exposing," and (ii) "constructing explicit exposing normals for all of the remaining cases." The construction of exposing normals "relies on a graded partition of the ground set $\mathcal{A}$" which "allows precise control over the relative magnitudes of the normal entries and reflects the combinatorial layering inherent in $\mathcal{A}$." The proof is "fully constructive" in that it provides "explicit normals certifying exposure of each ray." Concretely, the paper "establish[es] the negative part of the result" in Proposition~\ref{prop:unexposed}, then proves the remaining extreme rays are exposed in Propositions~\ref{prop:monomial} and~\ref{prop:nonmonomial}, and "finalize[s] the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:main}."

mcq.question: Let \(\mathcal{A}\subseteq \mathbb{N}^n\) be finite, and let \(r\) be an extreme ray of the SONC cone on \(\mathcal{A}\), i.e. the cone of sums of nonnegative circuit polynomials whose exponents lie in \(\mathcal{A}\). Here \(\mathbf{x}^\alpha=x_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots x_n^{\alpha_n}\), \(\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) denotes the ray generated by the monomial \(\mathbf{x}^\alpha\), an exponent \(\beta\) is called even if \(\beta\in (2\mathbb{N})^n\), and a circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) means that \(S\subseteq \mathcal{A}\cap (2\mathbb{N})^n\) is affinely independent, \(\beta\in \mathcal{A}\), and \(\beta\) lies in the relative interior of \(\operatorname{conv}(S)\). Which statement is equivalent to saying that \(r\) is not exposed (i.e. not an exposed face of the cone)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and there exists a circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) such that \(\alpha\in S\) and \(\beta\neq \alpha\) is even.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some even \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and there exists a circuit \((S,\alpha)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\), i.e. \(\alpha\) itself occurs as an inner exponent of a circuit on \(\mathcal{A}\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and there exists a circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) for which \(\alpha\in S\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and for every circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) with \(\alpha\in S\), the inner exponent \(\beta\) is even.

sketch:
A complete proof requires some further preparation, but the post-theorem introduction outlines these main steps.

1. **Assume** $\alpha\in(0,1/2]$, so $\alpha=\min\{\alpha,1-\alpha\}$.

2. **Incommensurable case** ($r_\alpha\notin\Q$): show that the *discrepancy* in $\alpha$-Kakutani tilings—“the fluctuation of the number of tiles that appear in large patches relative to their expected number (namely, the density)”—“can be shown to be large.” Combine this with “Laczkovich's criterion for uniform spreadness” to conclude that any associated Delone set “is not BD equivalent to a lattice,” hence is not uniformly spread. This is noted as a special case of Smilansky–Solomon, *[title and year not provided]*, Theorem 8.2.

3. **Commensurable case** ($r_\alpha\in\Q$): this is described as “rather different” and the “new result.”
   - **Cover by a standard primitive substitution**: “by introducing labelled prototiles and modifying the substitution rule in a suitable way,” construct “a standard primitive substitution that ‘covers’ the commensurable multiscale substitution construction,” meaning it generates substitution tilings “geometrically identical” to the $\alpha$-Kakutani tilings.
   - **Apply Solomon's criterion**: study uniform spreadness for the resulting primitive substitution tilings via “Solomon's criterion,” formulated as “a comparison between the leading Perron-Frobenius eigenvalue of the associated substitution matrix, and the subsequent eigenvalues.”
   - **Reduce to PV polynomial classification**: “In the special case considered here, this becomes a question of classification of certain Pisot–Vijayaraghavan (PV) polynomials,” and “from [this] the special values that appear in
\begin{equation}\label{eq:special values}
            r_\alpha:=\frac{\log(\min\{\alpha,1-\alpha\})}{\log(1-\min\{\alpha,1-\alpha\})}\in\left\{1,\frac{3}{2},2,3,4  \right\}.
    \end{equation}
can be extracted,” yielding exactly the listed set $\left\{1,\frac{3}{2},2,3,4\right\}$.

4. **Further remark beyond the theorem**: they add that this “scarcity” need not persist for more general multiscale substitution tilings (e.g. $(\alpha,\beta)$-Kakutani tilings), where they “present a countable set of parameters $(\alpha,\beta)$ for which the associated Delone sets are uniformly spread.”

Part (i) is obtained by “computing the Jacobian of the identity map from $(M_1 \times M_2,g)$ to $(M_1 \times M_2,g_+^t)$,” giving \eqref{eq:anest1}.

For (ii), “at a regular point of $\Sigma$ the $g$-unit exterior normal vector $\nu$ can be decomposed as $\nu=a\nu_1+b\nu_2$ so that $a^2+b^2=1$ and $\nu_1$, $\nu_2$ unit vectors tangent to $M_1$ and $M_2$, respectively.” The Jacobian of the identity map restricted to $\Sigma$ “equals $t^{m_2-1}(t^2a^2+b^2)^{1/2}$,” which for $t\le 1$ yields

  \hh_{g_+^t}^{m_1+m_2-1}(\Sg) 
  \leq t^{m_2-1}\,\hh_g^{m_1+m_2-1}(\Sg).
\end{equation}

For (iii), “$\nu$ is tangent to $M_2$ precisely when $a=0$ almost everywhere,” which characterizes when equality holds in the equation above.

theorem:
To prove the main theorem, let $X$ be an equidimensional projective scheme over a field $\mathbb{k}$.
	\label{thmA}
	Let $\mathscr{L}$ be an ample line bundle on $X$ and $W \subseteq Z$ be two closed subschemes of $X$.
	Then the following three conditions are equivalent:
	\begin{enumerate}[\;\;\rm (a)]
		\item $\II_W$ is integral over $\II_Z$.
		\item $s(Z, X) = s(W, X)$ viewed in $A_*(X)$.
		\item $\deg_\mathscr{L}\left(s^i(Z, X)\right) = \deg_\mathscr{L}\left(s^i(W, X)\right)$ for all $i \ge 0$.
	\end{enumerate}
sketch:
The post-theorem introduction indicates that, in establishing the main theorem, the proof \emph{relies on van Gastel’s foundational result} \cite{VanGastel}, which "establishes the connection between Fulton–MacPherson’s intersection theory" \cite{FULTON_INTER} and "St\"uckrad–Vogel’s intersection theory" \cite{VOGEL,FOV}. The stated \emph{starting point} is "van Gastel’s result expressing Segre classes in terms of Vogel cycles, and conversely expressing Vogel cycles in terms of Segre classes." The introduction further explains the strategy/advantage: "considering the Vogel cycle" allows one to apply "technical positivity results, including the one established in the following proposition." \begin{proposition}
	\label{prop_hard}
	Assume that $\Rees(\II_W)$ is not of finite type as a module over $\Rees(\II_Z)$.
	 Then we have the following strict inequality 
	 $$
	 \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{h^0\left(X, \II_W^{n}/\II_Z^{n} \otimes \LL^{\otimes 2pn} \right)}{n^d} \;>\; 0.
	 $$
\end{proposition} (The full proof is deferred: the proof of the main theorem is given later.)

mcq.question: Let $X$ be an equidimensional projective scheme over a field $\mathbb{k}$, let $\mathscr{L}$ be an ample line bundle on $X$, and let $W \subseteq Z$ be closed subschemes of $X$. Suppose (i) the ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}_W$ is integral over $\mathcal{I}_Z$, and (ii) the Segre classes satisfy $s(Z,X)=s(W,X)$ in the Chow group $A_*(X)$. Writing $s^i(Y,X)$ for the codimension-$i$ component of the Segre class $s(Y,X)$, and for $\alpha\in A_d(X)$ writing $\deg_{\mathscr{L}}(\alpha):=\deg\! ig(c_1(\mathscr{L})^d\smallfrown \alpha\big)$, which statement is equivalent to both (i) and (ii)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
It is equivalent to require that the alternating total \(\mathscr{L}\)-degree of the Segre classes agree, namely
\[

theorem:
\label{A}
Let $G$ be a $p$-solvable group with $P\in\Syl_p(G)$, and let $\mathcal{N}$ be a
normal $p$-series of $G$. Then the following hold.

{\rm (1)} $\Irr_0(G,\mathcal{N})=\Irr_{p'}(G)$ and
$\Irr_0({\bf N}_G(P),\mathcal{N})=\Irr_{p'}({\bf N}_G(P))$.

{\rm (2)} $|\Irr_i(G,\mathcal{N})|=|\Irr_i({\bf N}_G(P),\mathcal{N})|$ for all $i\ge 0$.

{\rm (3)} For $i\ge 0$, $\Irr_i(G,\mathcal{N})$ is nonempty
if and only if $p^i=\alpha(1)$ for some  $\alpha\in\Irr(P,\mathcal{N})$.

{\rm (4)} $|\Irr(G,\mathcal{N})|=|\Irr({\bf N}_G(P),\mathcal{N})|$.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem and Theorem \ref{B}, the authors say they "need to extend the definition of the map $\Psi:\Lin(P)\to\Irr(G)$" (Isaacs–Navarro) from linear characters to an arbitrary $\alpha\in\Irr(P)$. Using Corollary 3.15 and Theorem 3.16 of \cite{I2018}, they note that $\alpha$ "uniquely determines a pair $(W,\gamma)$", where $W$ is "the unique largest subgroup $G$ to which $\alpha$ extends" and $\gamma$ is "the unique $p$-special extension of $\alpha$ to $W$"; then they define the (extended) standard map by
\[
\Psi:\Irr(P)\to\Char(G),\quad \alpha\mapsto \gamma^G.
\]
They then state that the key ingredient "lies at the heart of the proof of the main theorem and Theorem \ref{B}", namely Theorem \ref{C}, which provides the structure needed: for $\alpha\in\Irr(P,\mathcal{N})$, $\Psi(\alpha)\in\Irr(G)$, and $\Psi(\alpha)=\Psi(\beta)$ exactly when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are ${\bf N}_G(P)$-conjugate; moreover, for each such $\alpha$ one forms the set
\[
\Irr_\alpha(G)=\{(\gamma\delta)^G\mid \delta\text{ is $p'$-special on }W\}\subseteq\Irr(G),
\]
with $\chi(1)_p=\alpha(1)$ for $\chi\in\Irr_\alpha(G)$, and these sets are either equal (when $\alpha,\beta$ are ${\bf N}_G(P)$-conjugate) or disjoint. The theorem also asserts the counting/covering statements
\[
|\Irr_\alpha(G)|=|\Irr({\bf N}_G(P)|\alpha)|,\qquad
\Irr(G,\mathcal{N})=\bigcup_{\alpha\in\Irr(P,\mathcal{N})}\Irr_\alpha(G),
\]
(and similarly a union decomposition for $\Irr({\bf N}_G(P),\mathcal{N})$). This is the described route: extend $\Psi$ via $(W,\gamma)$ and then apply Theorem \ref{C} to organize $\Irr(G,\mathcal{N})$ (and the corresponding normalizer set) into pieces indexed by $\Irr(P,\mathcal{N})$ with controlled degrees and matching cardinalities, and then conclude Theorem \ref{B}:
\begin{thmA}\label{B}
Let $G$ be a solvable group of odd order,
and let $P\in\Syl_p(G)$.
Suppose that $\NN$ is a normal $p$-series of $G$.
Then there is a canonical bijection from $\Irr(G,\NN)$ onto $\Irr({\bf N}_G(P),\NN)$.
Also, if $\xi\in\Irr({\bf N}_G(P),\NN)$ is the image of
$\chi\in\Irr(G,\NN)$ under this map, then
$\xi$ is a constituent of $\chi_{{\bf N}_G(P)}$
and $\xi(1)$ divides $\chi(1)$.

Furthermore, for each $i\ge 0$, the bijection
maps $\Irr_i(G,\NN)$ onto $\Irr_i({\bf N}_G(P),\NN)$,
and thus defines a canonical bijection between these two sets.
\end{thmA}

the approach is: (i) ``first make a reduction of the general problem to a certain restricted subset of hypergraphs''; (ii) for each hypergraph in this restricted subset, ``find a suitable permutation $\pi:[n]\rightarrow [n]$ such that the pair $(H, \pi(H))$, where $\pi(H)=\{\pi(T):\ T\in H\}$, violates the exchange axiom among the elements of $M(H)$.''

theorem:
\label{onto}
    Let an interval exchange transformation be defined by a sequence of parameters $\{a_i, c_i\}$ generated by the Rauzy cycle $\gamma^{a,c}$, such that $c_{i+1} = pa_i = p^2c_i$ for some integer $p \geq n + 1$. Such a transformation is non-uniquely ergodic.

    Specifically, it possesses $\floordown{\frac{n}{2}}$ distinct invariant ergodic measures, found as the limits
    $$\lambda_j = \lim_{m \to +\infty} \overline{\Theta}_{c_1,a_1}\overline{\Theta}_{c_2,a_2}...\overline{\Theta}_{c_m,a_m} e_j, \text{ for } j \in \{3, 5, \dots, 2\floordown{\frac{n}{2}}-1, n\}.$$,

Then, by applying these bounds and employing M. Keane’s method from \cite{Kn75}, they provide the proof of the main theorem.

sketch:
Outline of the proof of the main theorem: Let $G$ be almost simple with socle $T=\hbox{\rm PSU}(3,q)$, so $T\le G\le \hbox{\rm Aut}(T)$, $q=p^m$. Let $\alpha=M$ be a maximal subgroup of $G$ containing $M_0=\hbox{\rm PSO}(3,q)$ (up to conjugacy), and consider the primitive action of $G$ on the right cosets $[G\!:\!M]$. Then $q\ge 7$ and either (i) $G=T$ and $M=M_0$, or (ii) $G=T\ZZ_{m_1}$ and $M=M_0\ZZ_{m_1}$ with $m_1\mid 2m$ and $m_1\ne 1$. In case (i), a common neighbor of $\alpha$ and $\alpha^g$ in the Saxl graph (for any $g\in G$) is exhibited by a direct geometric construction developed earlier. This geometric construction does not extend to case (ii), so a new approach is developed later.

We first prove the following theorem.
\begin{theorem} \label{main1}  Let $G=\PSU(3,q)$ and $M=\PSO(3,q),$ a maximal subgroup of $G$, where $q=p^m\ge 7$ for an odd prime $p$.
Consider the primitive right multiplication action  of $G$ on the set of right cosets $\Omega =[G:M]$.  Then $b(G)=2$ and the BG-Conjecture holds.
  \end{theorem}

We also prove the following theorem.
\begin{theorem} \label{main2}  Let $G=\PSU(3,q)\ZZ_{m_1}$ and
    $\a=M=\PSO(3,q)\ZZ_{m_1}\le G$, with  $q=p^m\ge 7$ for an odd prime $p$,  $m_1\mid 2m$ and $m_1\ne 1$.
Under the primitive right multiplication action of $G$ on $\Omega=[G:M]$, we have $b(G)=2$ and the BG-Conjecture is true, provided $\sqrt{q}\geq 17$ or $45$ if $3\nmid (q+1)$ and $3\di (q+1)$, respectively.
  \end{theorem}

In establishing the main theorem, we combine Theorem~\ref{main1} and Theorem~\ref{main2}. The proof of Theorem~\ref{main2} depends crucially on estimates for $|\Gamma_r(\alpha)|$ and $|\Gamma_{nr}(\alpha)\cap \Gamma_{nr}(\alpha')|$, developed later, where $\Gamma_r(\alpha)$ (resp. $\Gamma_{nr}(\alpha)$) is the union of regular (resp. non-regular) suborbits of $G$ relative to $\alpha$.

sketch:
In order to prove the main theorem, the text says they "use constrained minimization methods".

Next, they "study the minimization of energy at fixed mass" and use the following result.

\begin{theo}\label{Th-EnergyGS}

    Let $d\in\{1,2\}$, $\lambda,\omega\in\R$, $\gamma>0$ and $A$ be the self-adjoint operator constructed in \cref{Sec-AndersonOp1d,Sec-AndersonOp2d} with $\xi$ a spatial white noise, $\D^{1,2}=\mathrm{D}(\sqrt{|A|})$ and $\D^{-1,2}$ its dual. For $u\in\D^{1,2}$, we define the mass as
    $$\mathcal{M}(u)=\frac{1}{2}\int_{\R^d}|u|^2\d x$$
    and the energy as
    $$\mathcal{E}(u)=\frac{1}{2}\langle -Au,u\rangle_{\D^{-1,2},\D^{1,2}}-\frac{\lambda}{2\gamma+2}\int_{\R^d}|u|^{2\gamma+2}\d x.$$
    Then, it holds
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item For $\lambda\leqslant0$, for any $m>0$, there exists $\phi_m\in\D^{1,2}$ such that $\phi_m>0$ and
        $$\argmin_{\substack{u\in\D^{1,2}\\\mathcal{M}(u)=m}}\mathcal{E}(u) = \left\{\e^{\complexI\theta}\phi_m,\; \theta\in\R\right\}.$$
        \item For $\lambda>0$ and $\gamma<\frac{2}{d}$, for any $m>0$, there exists $\phi_m\in\displaystyle\argmin_{u\in\D^{1,2},\;\mathcal{M}(u)=m}\mathcal{E}(u)$ such that $\phi_m>0$.
        \item For $\lambda>0$ and $\gamma=\frac{2}{d}$, there exists $m_{*,\lambda}(\Xi)>0$ such that for all $m\in(0,m_{*,\lambda}(\Xi))$, there exists $\phi_m\in\displaystyle\argmin_{u\in\D^{1,2},\;\mathcal{M}(u)=m}\mathcal{E}(u)$ such that $\phi_m>0$. Moreover, there exists $m_{*,\lambda}(\Xi)\leqslant m^*_\lambda(\Xi)\leqslant m^*_\lambda$ such that
        $$\forall m>m^*_\lambda(\Xi), \inf_{\substack{u\in\D^{1,2}\\\mathcal{M}(u)=m}}\mathcal{E}(u)=-\infty,$$
        where $m^*_\lambda$ is the critical mass of the classical nonlinear Schrdinger equation (i.e.\ without any potential).
        \item For $\lambda>0$ and $\gamma>\frac{2}{d}$, for any $m>0$,
        $$\inf_{\substack{u\in\D^{1,2}\\\mathcal{M}(u)=m}}\mathcal{E}(u)=-\infty.$$
    \end{enumerate}

\end{theo}

It is then stated that \cref{Prop-ExistenceEnergyGS,Prop-FrequencyEnergyGS} show that, for $\lambda\leqslant 0$ or $\gamma<\frac{2}{d}$, energy minimization at fixed mass provides positive solutions of
\begin{equation}
    -A\phi-\lambda|\phi|^{2\gamma}\phi+\omega \phi = 0. \label{Eq-StationaryAGP}
\end{equation}
for any possible value of $\omega$.

Later, they instead "investigate the minimization of action $\mathcal{S}_\omega=\mathcal{E}+\omega\mathcal{M}$". In the focusing case ($\lambda>0$), they note that "one easily verifies the action is not lowerbounded", so they "introduce the Nehari manifold" and obtain an action minimizer on it (Theorem~\ref{Th-ACtionGS}) for $\omega>-\mu_0$.

They also "stress out" that, in the presence of white noise, standing waves have "limited Sobolev regularity" (Theorem~\ref{Th-MaxRegSW}), giving the maximal regularity and a unique-continuation-type statement (if a solution lies in $\mathrm{H}^{2-\frac{d}{2},q}(U)$ on some open set $U$, then it "vanishes everywhere in $U$").

mcq.question:
Let $d\in\{1,2\}$, $\gamma>0$, and $\lambda,\omega\in\mathbb R$. Let $A$ be the self-adjoint operator associated with the Anderson Hamiltonian with spatial white noise $\xi$ in dimension $d$, let $\mu_0$ denote the lowest eigenvalue of $-A$, and let $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ be the domain of $\sqrt{|A|}$. Consider the equation
\[
-A\phi-\lambda|\phi|^{2\gamma}\phi+\omega\phi=0
\]
for $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$. Here, a positive solution means a solution with $\phi>0$, and a non-trivial non-negative solution means $\phi\ge 0$ and $\phi\not\equiv 0$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the exact criterion for existence of positive solutions, together with the corresponding nonexistence of non-trivial non-negative solutions for the remaining parameter regimes?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: A positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ exists precisely in the following three cases: $\lambda>0$ and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega<-\mu_0$. For every other choice of $(\lambda,\omega)$, there is no non-trivial non-negative solution in $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ exists precisely in the following three cases: $\lambda>0$, $\gamma<\tfrac{2}{d}$, and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega<-\mu_0$. For every other choice of parameters, there is no non-trivial non-negative solution in $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whenever one of the following three conditions holds, there exists a positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$: $\lambda>0$ and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega<-\mu_0$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ exists precisely in the following three cases: $\lambda>0$ and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega\le -\mu_0$. For every other choice of $(\lambda,\omega)$, there is no non-trivial non-negative solution in $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ exists precisely in the following three cases: $\lambda>0$ and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=-\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega<-\mu_0$. theorem:
\label{theorem: intro}
	Consistently from a measurable cardinal, there are two $\sigma$-complete ultrafilters $\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{W}$ such that $\mathcal{V}$ is Lipschitz below $\mathcal{W}$, but $\mathcal{V}$ and $\mathcal{W}$ are Ketonen-incomparable. Furthermore, the same is also consistent with the assumption that the Weak Ultrapower Axiom holds.

mcq.question: Assume the consistency strength of a measurable cardinal. A \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilter is an ultrafilter closed under countable intersections. Using the standard comparison notions \(<_L\) (the Lipschitz order) and \(<_k\) (the Ketonen order) on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters, where “Ketonen-incomparable” means that neither ultrafilter is below the other in \(<_k\), and writing WUA for the Weak Ultrapower Axiom, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about consistency phenomena for \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable; moreover, it is also consistent that such a situation occurs together with the Weak Ultrapower Axiom.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable; however, no model satisfying the Weak Ultrapower Axiom can contain such a pair.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\) and the Lipschitz and Ketonen orders do not coincide on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters; nevertheless, in every model of the Weak Ultrapower Axiom, any relation \(\mathcal V<_L\mathcal W\) already implies that \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are comparable in the Ketonen order.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that the Lipschitz and Ketonen orders on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters can fail to coincide, but only outside the Weak Ultrapower Axiom; under WUA, the Ketonen order is always linear on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters.

theorem:
\label{thm-main}
	Let $X$ be a Euclidean  building.  Let $C\subset X$ be a convex subset of $X$
	which is isometric to a convex subset of some Euclidean space.  Then $C$ is contained in an apartment $A$  of $X$.  

	Moreover, if for some $x\in C$ we choose an apartment  $A^x$  in the infinitesimal Euclidean building  $T_xX$ with $T_xC\subset A^x$ then $A$ can be chosen so that $T _xA=A^x$.

To make this enlargement procedure work, they deal with the case of convex polyhedra $C$ first, and then deduce the result for general convex subsets by an appropriate approximation. They also note (contrasting with the spherical case) that in the Euclidean case one cannot rely on the finiteness of the simplicial decomposition of spherical apartments, which in the spherical setting allows for a choice of a potential enlargement $A$ of the convex subset $C$.

theorem:
\label{main}

Every countable (recursively presented) group embeds into a (finitely presented) group $\Gamma$ with property $\mathrm{(T)}$, that is Frobenius stable but does not have property $\mathrm{(T_2)}$. In fact, $\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma; V) \neq 0$ for \emph{every} unitary $\Gamma$-representation $V$.
sketch:
To prove \cref{main}, one uses that “every asymptotic homomorphism is close to one that factors through an approximable quotient … Therefore groups with no non-trivial approximable quotients are (vacuously) stable. \cref{main} exploits this observation.”

In the proof: given a countable (recursively presented) group $C$, “up to taking a free product with $\mathbb{Z}$, may assume that $C$ has no non-trivial finite normal subgroups.” Choose “a non-Frobenius approximable group $A$ … a finite central extension of a finitely presented linear group,” hence with solvable word problem. Then “combining \cref{no approx quotients} and \cref{one rel quotients}, we obtain a (finitely presented) group $\Gamma$ that has property $\mathrm{(T)}$, verifies the strong negation of property $\mathrm{(T_2)}$ (indeed $\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma;V)\neq 0$ for every unitary representation $V$), and has no Frobenius approximable quotients, in particular it is Frobenius stable.”

mcq.question: Let \(G\) be a countable recursively presented group (that is, a group given by a countable recursive presentation). Here \(\Gamma\) is required to be a finitely presented group; property \(\mathrm{(T)}\) means Kazhdan's property \(\mathrm{(T)}\); property \(\mathrm{(T_2)}\) means that \(\operatorname{H}^i(\Gamma;V)=0\) for every unitary \(\Gamma\)-representation \(V\) and \(i\in\{1,2\}\); and "Frobenius stable" means stable with respect to asymptotic homomorphisms into finite-dimensional unitary groups equipped with the Frobenius norm. Also, \(\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma;V)\) denotes the second cohomology of \(\Gamma\) with coefficients in a unitary \(\Gamma\)-representation \(V\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a finitely presented group \(\Gamma\) into which \(G\) embeds?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a finitely presented group \(\Gamma\) into which \(G\) embeds such that \(\Gamma\) has property \(\mathrm{(T)}\), is Frobenius stable, does not have property \(\mathrm{(T_2)}\), and in fact satisfies \(\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma;V) \neq 0\) for every unitary \(\Gamma\)-representation \(V\).

choices.label: B

mcq.question:
Let $\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^N$ ($N\ge 3$) be a bounded open set, let $J=\{1,\dots,N\}$, and write $D_j=\partial_{x_j}$. Assume $q>0$, $0<\theta<1$, $f\in L^1(\Omega)$ with $f\ge 0$ and $f\not\equiv 0$, and numbers $p_1,\dots,p_N$ satisfy $2\le p_1\le \cdots \le p_N$ and $2\le \overline p:=N\left(\sum_{j\in J}\frac1{p_j}\right)^{-1}<N$. Assume also that the measurable coefficients satisfy $0<\alpha\le a(x)\le \beta$ and $0<\mu\le b(x)\le \nu$. Consider the boundary value problem
\[
\begin{cases}
-\displaystyle\sum_{j\in J} D_j\!\left(\bigl[a(x)+u^q\bigr]|D_j u|^{p_j-2}D_j u\right)+b(x)\displaystyle\sum_{j\in J}\frac{|D_j u|^{p_j}}{u^{\theta}}=f & \text{in }\Omega,\\[2mm]
u>0 & \text{in }\Omega,\\
u=0 & \text{on }\partial\Omega.
\end{cases}
\]
Here $W_0^{1,(s_j)}(\Omega)$ denotes the anisotropic Sobolev space of zero-trace functions with $D_j u\in L^{s_j}(\Omega)$ for each $j$, and a distributional solution is understood in the usual weak sense for this problem. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence and anisotropic Sobolev regularity of positive distributional solutions of this problem?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The following piecewise existence result holds: if $0<q<1-\theta$, then there exists a distributional solution $u\in W_0^{1,(r_j)}(\Omega)$, where $r_j=\dfrac{N(\overline p-\theta)}{\overline p(N-\theta)}p_j$ for every $j\in J$; if $1-\theta<q\le 1$, then there exists a distributional solution $u\in W_0^{1,(\eta_j)}(\Omega)$ such that $r_j<\dfrac{N(\overline p-1+q)}{\overline p(N+q-1)}p_j$ for every $j\in J$; and if $q>1$, then there exists a distributional solution $u\in W_0^{1,(p_j)}(\Omega)$.

choices.label: B

choices.label: D
choices.text: The following piecewise existence result holds: if $0<q<1-\theta$, then there exists a distributional solution $u\in W_0^{1,(r_j)}(\Omega)$, where $r_j=\dfrac{N(\overline p-\theta)}{\overline p(N-\theta)}p_j$ for every $j\in J$; if $1-\theta<q\le 1$, then there exists a distributional solution $u\in W_0^{1,(s_j)}(\Omega)$, where $s_j=\dfrac{N(\overline p-1+q)}{\overline p(N+q-1)}p_j$ for every $j\in J$; and if $q>1$, then there exists a distributional solution $u\in W_0^{1,(p_j)}(\Omega)$.

choices.label: E

theorem:
\label{A}
There exists a continuum sized family $\{G_x\}_{x\in \{0,1\}^\mathbb N}$ of pairwise non-measure equivalent  ICC countable groups
 which have property (T) (and hence are finitely generated).

Moreover, each $G_x$ satisfies $\beta^{(2)}_k(G_x)=0$, for every $k\geq 1$, and can be taken torsion-free.,

mcq.question: Here, two countable groups are called measure equivalent if they admit commuting actions on an infinite measure space such that both actions have fundamental domains of finite measure. theorem:
\label{thm:main_window}
Let $u\ge 1$, $q\ge 2$ be integers, and let $\mathcal F$ be a finite family of
non-empty binary words.  Let $L=\max\{|f|:f\in\mathcal F\}$ and fix $M\ge L$.
Then the set
\[
   S_u^{(M)}=\{n\ge 0:\ uq^n\in \mathcal K_{\mathcal F}^{(M)}\}
\]
is either finite or ultimately periodic.  That is, there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$
such that
\[
   n\in S_u^{(M)}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad(n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a pair $(n_0,p)$ and decide whether
$S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite.  In the finite case, the same procedure
outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.
sketch: To prove the main theorem, the argument is “entirely finite-state”. The key input is that “multiplication by a fixed integer $q$ in the Fibonacci numeration system is realizable by a finite sequential transducer”, and when “reading digits from the least significant side” this yields (for each $M$) “a deterministic update rule on the set of length-$M$ windows $\{0,1,\#\}^M$.” Composing this with “the finite automaton that checks avoidance of $\mathcal F$ inside the window” produces “a deterministic dynamical system on a finite state space.” The orbit corresponding to $(uq^n)_{n\ge 0}$ is thus “eventually periodic”, and “which yields ultimate periodicity of $S_u^{(M)}$ as an immediate corollary.” Finally, “the same finite-state construction gives an explicit algorithm to compute the eventual period” (hence to compute $(n_0,p)$ and decide finiteness, and list elements in the finite case).

Let $(F_n)_{n\ge 0}$ be the Fibonacci numbers, defined by $F_0=0$, $F_1=1$, and $F_{n+2}=F_{n+1}+F_n$. Every integer $N\ge 1$ has a unique Zeckendorf representation
\[
N=\sum_{i=2}^k \varepsilon_i F_i,
\]
where $\varepsilon_i\in\{0,1\}$, $\varepsilon_k=1$, and $\varepsilon_i\varepsilon_{i+1}=0$. Write $\operatorname{rev}(Z(N))=\varepsilon_2\varepsilon_3\cdots \varepsilon_k$ for the Zeckendorf digits read from least significant to most significant, and let $\widetilde Z(N)=\operatorname{rev}(Z(N))\,\#^{\omega}$, where $\#\notin\{0,1\}$ is a padding symbol.

Let $u\ge 1$ and $q\ge 2$ be integers, let $\mathcal F$ be a finite family of non-empty binary words, let $L=\max\{|f|:f\in\mathcal F\}$, and fix $M\ge L$. Define
\[
\mathcal K_{\mathcal F}^{(M)}=\{N\ge 1:\text{ the prefix of length }M\text{ of }\widetilde Z(N)\text{ contains no factor from }\mathcal F\},
\]
and
\[
S_u^{(M)}=\{n\ge 0: u q^n\in \mathcal K_{\mathcal F}^{(M)}\}.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $S_u^{(M)}$?

$S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a pair $(n_0,p)$ and decide whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite; in the finite case, the same procedure outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.

\[
\mathcal A(z,\xi)\cdot \xi\ge \nu\big(|\xi|^p+a(z)|\xi|^q\big),
\qquad
|\mathcal A(z,\xi)|\le L\big(|\xi|^{p-1}+a(z)|\xi|^{q-1}\big)
\]

\[
 u\in C(0,T;L^2(\Omega,\mathbb R^N))\cap L^q(0,T;W^{1,q}(\Omega,\mathbb R^N))\cap L^\infty(\Omega_T)
\]
is a weak solution in \(\Omega_T\) of
\[
 u_t-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(z,\nabla u)=-\operatorname{div}\big(|F|^{p-2}F+a(z)|F|^{q-2}F\big).

$$d_0^{-1}\le f_p\le d_0,\qquad [f_p]_{C^\alpha(\mathbb{R}^n)}\le d_0,\qquad f_p(x+a_i e_i)=f_p(x)
\ \text{ for all }x\in\mathbb{R}^n,$$
for $i=1,\dots,n$, and for some bounded open set $\mathcal O\subset\mathbb{R}^n$,
$$f\in C(\mathbb{R}^n)\cap C^{\alpha}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\overline{\mathcal O}),$$
and, for every $x\in\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$,
$$|x|^{\beta}|(f-f_p)(x)|+|x|^{\beta+\alpha}[f-f_p]_{C^{\alpha}(\overline{B_{|x|/2}(x)})}\le d_1.$$ 
Also, let $\mathcal A$ denote the set of real symmetric positive definite $n\times n$ matrices $A$ such that

Let $\mathrm{Fl}_C(n)$ be the type $C_n$ flag manifold, i.e. the variety of isotropic flags
\[
0\subset V_1\subset\cdots\subset V_n\subset \mathbb C^{2n}
\]
with respect to a fixed symplectic form, and let
\[
0=\mathcal S_0\subset \mathcal S_1\subset\cdots\subset \mathcal S_n\subset \underline{\mathbb C}^{2n}
\]
be its tautological filtration of subbundles, with $\operatorname{rk}(\mathcal S_k)=k$. In the $T$-equivariant quantum $K$-ring $QK_T(\mathrm{Fl}_C(n))[y]$, write $\lambda_y(E)=\sum_i[\wedge^iE]y^i$, let $\star$ denote the quantum product, $E^\vee$ the dual bundle, $[E]$ the $K$-theory class of a bundle $E$, and let $Q_1,\dots,Q_n$ be the Novikov variables, with the conventions $Q_0=0$ and $\mathcal S_{-1}=0$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about these objects?

correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
In $QK_T(\mathrm{Fl}_C(n))[y]$, for $1\le k\le n$, one has
\[
\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-1})\star\lambda_y(\mathcal S_k/\mathcal S_{k-1})
=\lambda_y(\mathcal S_k)-y\frac{Q_{k-1}}{1-Q_k}[\mathcal S_k/\mathcal S_{k-1}]\star\bigl(\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-1})-\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-2})\bigr),
\]
and likewise
\[
\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-1}^{\vee})\star\lambda_y((\mathcal S_k/\mathcal S_{k-1})^{\vee})
=\lambda_y(\mathcal S_k^{\vee})-y\frac{Q_{k-1}}{1-Q_k}[(\mathcal S_k/\mathcal S_{k-1})^{\vee}]\star\bigl(\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-1}^{\vee})-\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-2}^{\vee})\bigr).
\]

\[
\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-1})\star\lambda_y(\mathcal S_k/\mathcal S_{k-1})
=\lambda_y(\mathcal S_k)-y\frac{Q_{k-1}}{1-Q_{k-1}}[\mathcal S_k/\mathcal S_{k-1}]\star\bigl(\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-1})-\lambda_y(\mathcal S_{k-2})\bigr).

theorem:
\label{t:main}
  Let $q\ge4$ be a prime power and $G$ be almost simple with socle
  $S=\PSL_2(q)$, that is $S\le G\le\aut(S)$. Suppose that $G$ acts
  primitively on $\Omega$. Then for every distinct
  $\alpha, \beta\in\Omega$ there exists $s\in S$ such that
  $\alpha^s=\beta$ and $s$ fixes no point of $\Omega$.
sketch: To prove the main theorem, one uses that when $G$ acts primitively on $\Omega$, “the possibilities for a point stabilizer $S_\omega < S$ are known.” The argument then splits by the type of $S_\omega$: “some cases are relatively easy to handle,” while the “more difficult ones arise when $S_\omega$ is dihedral, or when $S_\omega$ is one of the polyhedral groups $A_4$, $S_4$, or $A_5$.” In the dihedral case, the proof “require[s] Hasse’s bound on the number of points on elliptic curves over finite fields,” and to exclude the polyhedral cases one “derive[s] trace identities for the binary polyhedral groups $2A_4$, $2S_4$, and $2A_5$.” The authors note that “in some cases there are lengthy calculations” and they “provide SageMath code at \cite{dera_psl2_verify} that confirms the claims.”

mcq.question: Let q\ge 4 be a prime power, let G be an almost simple group with socle S=\mathrm{PSL}_2(q) (so S\le G\le \mathrm{Aut}(S)), and suppose G acts primitively on a set \Omega. Here, for g\in G and \omega\in\Omega, \omega^g denotes the image of \omega under the action. For distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of an element sending \alpha to \beta while fixing no point of \Omega?

correct_choice.label: A

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every pair of distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, there exists an element g\in G such that \alpha^g=\beta and g fixes no point of \Omega.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every pair of distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, there exists an element s\in S such that \alpha^s=\beta and every nontrivial power of s fixes no point of \Omega.

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a single conjugacy class C\subseteq S such that for every pair of distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, some element s\in C satisfies \alpha^s=\beta and s fixes no point of \Omega.

theorem:
\label{t1}
    For any $k\in \mathbb{N}$ and $2$-coloring of $\mathbb{N},$ there exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $\{x,y,xy, x+iy: i\leq k\}$ is monochromatic.,
sketch:
The authors state that “the basic strategy used in the proof of the main theorem … is essentially as follows:”
\begin{enumerate}
\item “First, show that the result holds when the color of the step size $y$ is ignored.” Then one finds monochromatic sets $\{x,xy,x+iy: i\leq k\}$, “which can be done either through Moreira's work … or the first author's …”.
\item “Define an appropriate structure vs randomness dichotomy, and argue that a $2$-coloring must either be structured or random.”
\item “Argue that in either case the extra information we obtain can be used to upgrade the proof from step (1) to control the color of the $y$ term as well.”
\end{enumerate}
They further note that they “give two proofs of the main theorem based on the two ways of handling step (1) and using two different structure vs randomness dichotomies.”

\]

sketch:
To establish the main theorem (2) it suffices to prove the local statement

\begin{proposition}\label{proposition:1.2}
Let $\mathcal{G}_k$ be the gauge group of a principal $G_2$-bundle over $S^4$ whose classifying map is $k\in \mathbb{Z}\cong \pi_{4}(BG_2)$.
Then, we have 
\begin{itemize}
\item[{\rm (1)}] $\mathcal{G}_k\simeq_{(2)} \mathcal{G}_{k'}$  if $(k, 4)=(k', 4)$, 
\item[{\rm (2)}] 
$\mathcal{G}_k\simeq_{(3)} \mathcal{G}_{k'}$  if $(k, 3)=(k', 3)$, 
\item[{\rm (3)}] $\mathcal{G}_k\simeq_{(7)} \mathcal{G}_{k'}$  if $(k, 7)=(k', 7)$
and 
\item[{\rm (4)}] $\mathcal{G}_k\simeq_{(p)} \mathcal{G}_{k'} \simeq _{(p)} G_2 \times \Omega_0^4 G_2$ for $p\not=2,3,7$.
\end{itemize}
\end{proposition}

The paper reduces this to proving the preceding proposition (1) (the $2$-local case), since (2),(3),(4) are cited from \cite{ktt-2017}.

The method recalled is: using Gottlieb’s identification $B\mathcal{G}_k\simeq \mathrm{Map}(S^4,BG)_k$ and the resulting fiber sequence
\[
\mathcal{G}_k\to G\xrightarrow{\partial_k} \Omega_0^3 G\to B\mathcal{G}_k\to BG,
\]
so $\mathcal{G}_k$ is the homotopy fiber of $\partial_k$. By Lang, $\partial_k$ is the triple adjoint of the Samelson product $\langle k\cdot i_3,1\rangle$, hence by linearity $\langle k\cdot i_3,1\rangle\simeq k\cdot\langle i_3,1\rangle$. Theriault’s result is then invoked: $\mathcal{G}_k\simeq_{(p)}\mathcal{G}_{k'}$ for all primes $p$ if $(k,m)=(k',m)$ where $m$ is the order of $\langle i_3,1\rangle$.

For $G=G_2$ at $p=2$, \cite{ktt-2017} gives that the order of $\langle i_3,1\rangle$ in $[\Sigma^3G_2,G_2]_{(2)}$ is at least $4$, so to show the preceding proposition (1), it suffices to prove the following.

\begin{proposition}\label{proposition:1.3}  The order of the Samelson product $\langle i_3, 1\rangle$ in $[\Sigma^3 G_2, G_2]_{(2)}$ is at most $4$.
\end{proposition}

The proof sketch for the preceding proposition is given: using that $3\cdot\langle i_3,1\rangle$ factors through $\Sigma^3q\colon \Sigma^3G_2\to \Sigma^3V$ (with $V=G_2/SU(2)$), write
\[
3\cdot\langle i_3,1\rangle\simeq \psi\circ \Sigma^3q.
\]
With the homotopy decomposition $\Sigma^3V\simeq P^9(2)\vee S^{14}$, choose maps $s'\colon \Sigma^3V\to P^9(2)$ and $s''\colon S^{14}\to \Sigma^3V$ so that
\[
\mathrm{id}_{\Sigma^3V}\simeq \Sigma^3i_6\circ s'\; +\; s''\circ \Sigma^3p_{11}.
\]
Then
\[
3\cdot\langle i_3,1\rangle\simeq \psi\circ \Sigma^3i_6\circ s'\circ \Sigma^3q\; +\; \psi\circ s''\circ \Sigma^3(p_{11}\circ q).
\]
Using \cite{ktt-2017} (Lemma 5.2(a),(b)) that “the order of the identity map of $P^9(2)$ is $4$,” one gets $4(\Sigma^3i_6\circ s')\simeq 0$. The remaining summand is controlled by

\begin{proposition} \label{proposition:1.5}
We have  $4(\psi\circ s'')\simeq 0$  in $\pi_{14}(G_2)_{(2)}$. \end{proposition}

Combining these shows that the order of $\langle i_3,1\rangle$ in $[\Sigma^3G_2,G_2]_{(2)}$ is at most $4$, hence the preceding proposition (1), and therefore the main theorem (2) via the Samelson-product/Thériault criterion.

(They additionally note that with the proposition above and results of \cite{ktt-2017}, the global order is computed as $84$ (Theorem~\ref{theorem:1.4}).)

mcq.question: Let $G_2$ be the compact exceptional Lie group, and let $BG_2$ denote its classifying space. For integers $k$ and $k'$, let $\mathcal{G}_k$ denote the gauge group (the topological group of bundle automorphisms over $S^4$) of the principal $G_2$-bundle over $S^4$ classified by $k\in \pi_4(BG_2)\cong \mathbb{Z}$. Writing $(a,b)$ for the greatest common divisor and $\simeq_{(p)}$ for $p$-local homotopy equivalence, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the homotopy types of $\mathcal{G}_k$ and $\mathcal{G}_{k'}$?

theorem:
\label{thm:main}
Let $\mu$ be a signed Radon measure on $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $k \in \{0,1,\dots,n\}$. Then $\mathcal{S}_\mu^k$ is contained in a countable union of $k$-dimensional Lipschitz graphs. Moreover, the approximate tangent plane to $\mathcal{S}_\mu^k$ at $x$ coincides with $D_x$ for $\mathcal{H}^{k}$-a.e.\ $x \in \mathcal{S}_\mu^k$.,
sketch:
The post-theorem introduction gives the following proof idea for the main theorem.

1. **Cone containment near each point.** For every $x\in\mathcal{S}_\mu^k$, there exists $r>0$ such that the points in $\mathcal{S}_\mu^k\cap B_r(x)$ with similar properties are contained in a cone $x+C$ around $x+D_x$.

2. **Contradiction setup if cone containment fails.** Otherwise, one can find a sequence $y_j\notin x+C$ with $r_j=|x-y_j|\to 0$ such that the measures $c_j\mu_{y_j}$ and $\mu_{y_j,r_j}$ are “as close as we want.”

3. **Two limiting descriptions of the same asymptotically close measures.**
   - On the one hand, $c_j\mu_{y_j}$ converges to a measure $\nu$ whose invariant subspace is close to $D_x$.
   - On the other hand, $\mu_{y_j,r_j}$ are translations of $\mu_{x,r_j}$ “with respect to points outside $C$,” hence they converge to a translation of $\mu_x$ whose invariant subspace is not contained in $C$.

4. **Contradiction.** This “contradict[s] the closeness of $c_j\mu_{y_j}$ and $\mu_{y_j,r_j}$,” forcing the desired cone property; this yields the Lipschitz-graph covering and the identification of the approximate tangent plane with $D_x$ for $\mathcal{H}^k$-a.e. $x\in\mathcal{S}_\mu^k$.

mcq.question:
Let \(\mu\) be a signed Radon measure on \(\mathbb{R}^n\). Say that \(\mu\) has a unique blow-up at \(x\) if there exists a Radon measure \(\mu_x\) such that, for every sequence \(r_j\to 0^+\), after passing to a subsequence one has
\[
\mu_{x,r_j}:=\frac{1}{|\mu|(B_{r_j}(x))}(\tau_{x,r_j})_\#\mu \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} c\,\mu_x
\]
for some constant \(c\neq 0\), where \(\tau_{x,r}(y)=(y-x)/r\) and \(|\mu|\) is the total variation measure of \(\mu\). For points \(x\) with a unique blow-up, let \(D_x\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) denote the distinguished linear subspace associated with that blow-up, and for \(k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}\) define the \(k\)-stratum by
\[
\mathcal S_\mu^k:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n:\text{\(\mu\) has a unique blow-up at \(x\) and }\dim D_x=k\}.
\]
A \(k\)-dimensional Lipschitz graph means the graph of a Lipschitz map from a \(k\)-dimensional affine subspace into its orthogonal complement. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the structure of \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) and its tangent planes under the assumptions above?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) is contained in a countable union of \(k\)-dimensional Lipschitz graphs, and for \(\mathcal H^k\)-almost every \(x\in \mathcal S_\mu^k\), the approximate tangent \(k\)-plane to \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) at \(x\) exists and is exactly \(D_x\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) has the property that, for every \(R>0\), the set \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\cap B_R(0)\) is contained in a finite union of \(k\)-dimensional Lipschitz graphs, and for \(\mathcal H^k\)-almost every \(x\in \mathcal S_\mu^k\), the approximate tangent \(k\)-plane to \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) at \(x\) exists and is exactly \(D_x\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) is contained in a countable union of \(k\)-dimensional Lipschitz graphs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) is contained in a countable union of \(k\)-dimensional Lipschitz graphs, and for every \(x\in \mathcal S_\mu^k\), the approximate tangent \(k\)-plane to \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) at \(x\) exists and is exactly \(D_x\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) is contained in a countable union of \(k\)-dimensional Lipschitz graphs, and for \(\mathcal H^k\)-almost every \(x\in \mathcal S_\mu^k\), the approximate tangent \(k\)-plane to \(\operatorname{supp}\mu\) at \(x\) exists and is exactly \(D_x\).

theorem:
\label{conj:PTB}
Let $\pi$ be a generic representation of $G$, and $\phi=\phi_\pi$.
\begin{itemize}
\item[(1)] If $\pi$ is $E$-distinguished, then $\phi$ takes values in $\Sp_n(\mathbb{C})$.
\item[(2)] Suppose $\phi$ takes values in $\Sp_n(\mathbb{C})$ and we write it in the following form:
    \[
    \phi = \bigoplus_{i\in I_\phi} \phi_i \oplus \bigoplus_{j\in J_\phi}(\phi_j\oplus\phi_j^\vee),
    \]
where $I_\phi,  J_\phi$ are finite sets and $\phi_k\,\colon WD_F\to\GL_{n_k}(\mathbb{C})$ is a discrete $L$-parameter such that
\begin{itemize}
\item for each $i\in I_\phi$,  $n_i$ is even and $\phi_i$ takes values in $\Sp_{n_i}(\mathbb{C})$,
\item for any $i,  i'\in I_\phi$,  we have $\phi_i\not\simeq\phi_{i'}$ if $i\neq i'$. 
\end{itemize}
Then $\pi$ is $E$-distinguished if and only if we have 
    \[
    \e_E(\phi_i) = (-1)^{n_i/d},   \qquad  i\in I_\phi.
    \]
\end{itemize}

mcq.question:
Let \(F\) be a finite extension of \(\mathbb{Q}_p\), let \(D\) be a central division \(F\)-algebra of dimension \(d^2\), let \(m\ge 1\), and set \(G=\mathrm{GL}_m(D)\). Let \(n=md\), assume \(n\) is even, let \(E/F\) be a quadratic extension, and let \(H\) be the centralizer of \(E^\times\) in \(G\). A smooth representation \(\pi\) of \(G\) is called \(E\)-distinguished if \(\operatorname{Hom}_H(\pi,\mathbb{C})\neq 0\). Let \(\pi\) be a generic representation of \(G\), and let \(\phi=\phi_\pi\) be its \(L\)-parameter, viewed as a representation of the Weil-Deligne group \(WD_F\). Assume that \(\phi\) takes values in \(\mathrm{Sp}_n(\mathbb{C})\) and can be written as
\[
\phi = \bigoplus_{i\in I_\phi} \phi_i \oplus \bigoplus_{j\in J_\phi}(\phi_j\oplus\phi_j^\vee),
\]
where \(I_\phi\) and \(J_\phi\) are finite sets, each \(\phi_k\colon WD_F\to\mathrm{GL}_{n_k}(\mathbb{C})\) is a discrete \(L\)-parameter, for every \(i\in I_\phi\) the integer \(n_i\) is even and \(\phi_i\) takes values in \(\mathrm{Sp}_{n_i}(\mathbb{C})\), and \(\phi_i\not\simeq \phi_{i'}\) whenever \(i\neq i'\) in \(I_\phi\). Here \(\phi_j^\vee\) denotes the contragredient dual of \(\phi_j\). For such symplectic \(\phi_i\), write
\[
\varepsilon_E(\phi_i):=\eta_{E/F}(-1)^{n_i/2}\,\epsilon\bigl(1/2,\phi_i|_{WD_E},\psi_E\bigr),
\]
where \(\eta_{E/F}\) is the quadratic character of \(F^\times\) attached to \(E/F\), \(\psi_E=\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{E/F}\) for a nontrivial character \(\psi:F\to\mathbb{C}^\times\), and \(\epsilon\) is the Deligne-Langlands epsilon factor. Under these assumptions, which statement about the \(E\)-distinguished property of \(\pi\) is valid?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^m\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: If \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished, then \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^{n_i/d}\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^{n_i/d}\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\) such that \(n_i/d\) is odd; no further condition is required for the remaining indices in \(I_\phi\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\eta_{E/F}(-1)^{n/2}\,\epsilon\bigl(1/2,\phi|_{WD_E},\psi_E\bigr)=(-1)^m\).

sketch: Key in both arguments is the Ahlswede-Daykin four function theorem (adapted to Euclidean setting in the context of the main conjecture). The authors "sketch the easy fact that the classical Ahlswede-Daykin four function theorem, stated for a finite lattice, implies the Euclidean version ... that we utilize in the proof of the main conjecture," and they also investigate "a Transport based proof for the result in $\mathbb{R}^d$" ("drawing on \cite{GRST21}"). More broadly, they confirm the main conjecture by proving a more general statement: convolution with $g$ preserves log-supermodularity when $g$ is "a log-concave product density," and they "prove that a discrete analog holds for the integer lattice as well."

mcq.question:
Let
\[
g(x)=(2\pi)^{-d/2}e^{-|x|^2/2},\qquad x\in\mathbb{R}^d,
\]
be the standard Gaussian density on \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Let \(f\in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) be log-supermodular, meaning that for all \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}^d\),
\[
f(x\vee y)f(x\wedge y)\ge f(x)f(y),
\]
where \((x\vee y)_i=\max\{x_i,y_i\}\) and \((x\wedge y)_i=\min\{x_i,y_i\}\). Define the convolution
\[
(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} f(x-t)g(t)\,dt.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the log-supermodularity of \(f*g\) under these hypotheses?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The convolution \(f*g\) is log-supermodular for smooth positive log-supermodular \(f\), but this need not persist for arbitrary \(L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) log-supermodular \(f\); the \(L_1\) hypothesis alone is too weak.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The convolution \(f*g\) is log-supermodular whenever \(f\in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) is log-supermodular.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The Gaussian kernel is genuinely exceptional: there exists a log-concave product density \(h(x)=\prod_{i=1}^d h_i(x_i)\) and a log-supermodular \(f\in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) for which \(f*h\) is not log-supermodular.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Among log-concave product densities, the property that \(f*h\) is log-supermodular for every log-supermodular \(f\in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) characterizes the Gaussian kernel, up to translation and diagonal rescaling.

sketch:
Our proofs rely on Dirac operator methods. A fundamental ingredient is the relative index theorem for higher indices due to Xie and Yu (Theorem~A in Xie--Yu, \emph{[title not provided in ref\_dict]} (2014)). Inspired by the approaches in Su--Zhang, \emph{[title not provided in ref\_dict]}, and Wang--Xie--Linf, \emph{[title not provided in ref\_dict]}, we construct an explicit representative of the relative higher index and compute it in the $K$-theory of the group $C^*$-algebra. We also provide a Dirac-operator-based proof of the Ricci-flatness statement, following ideas similar to those in Wang--Zhu, \emph{[title not provided in ref\_dict]}. (In establishing the main theorem, we treat it as a special case of
\begin{theorem}\label{thm:main}
	Let $M^n$ be a closed aspherical manifold and $\Omega\subset M$ a smooth region. Assume that
	$$\pi_1(\Omega)\to \pi_1(M)\textup{ is the trivial map}.$$
	Let $N^n$ be a spin manifold with boundary, and $i\colon \partial N\to \partial \Omega$ a diffeomorphism preserving the spin structure\footnote{Although $M$ itself need not be spin, the triviality of $\pi_1(\Omega)\to\pi_1(M)$ implies that $\Omega$ and $\partial\Omega$ inherit a canonical spin structure from the spin universal cover $\widetilde M$.}. Set $Z\coloneqq (M\backslash\Omega)\cup_{\partial\Omega} N$. If the rational strong Novikov conjecture holds for $\pi_1(M)$, then $Z$ does not carry any complete metric with positive scalar curvature. Furthermore, any complete metric with non-negative scalar curvature on $Z$ is Ricci-flat.
\end{theorem}
by choosing $\Omega$ to be a small ball in $M$.)

Write $\Gamma=\pi_1(M)$, and assume that the rational strong Novikov conjecture holds for $\Gamma$, i.e. the assembly map $\mu\colon K_*(M)\otimes \mathbb{Q}\to K_*(C_r^*(\Gamma))\otimes \mathbb{Q}$ is injective. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about complete Riemannian metrics on $M\# N$ under these hypotheses?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The connected sum $M\# N$ admits no complete metric of positive scalar curvature. Moreover, if $M\# N$ carries a complete metric of non-negative scalar curvature, then that metric must be Ricci-flat.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The connected sum $M\# N$ admits no complete metric of positive scalar curvature. Moreover, every complete metric on $M\# N$ with non-negative scalar curvature is in fact flat.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every complete metric on $M\# N$ with non-negative scalar curvature has identically zero scalar curvature; in particular, $M\# N$ admits no complete metric of positive scalar curvature.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The connected sum $M\# N$ admits no complete metric of non-negative scalar curvature at all; equivalently, $M\# N$ carries neither a complete Ricci-flat metric nor a complete metric of positive scalar curvature.

choices.label: E
choices.text: If $M\# N$ carries one complete metric of non-negative scalar curvature, then every complete metric on $M\# N$ must also have non-negative scalar curvature and be Ricci-flat.

theorem:
\label{th:BC:u}
	If the underlying probability space $(\Omega,\mathscr{F},\mathrm{P})$ is 
	complete, then:
	\begin{enumerate}[\noindent (1)]
	\item There exists a strictly decreasing, convex, 
		and continuous function $\lambda: (0\,,\infty) \to (0\,,\infty)$, 
		independent of $\sigma$, such that $\lim_{r\to0^+} \lambda(r) = \infty$, 
		$\lim_{r \to \infty} \lambda(r) = 0$, and 
		\[
			\mathrm{P}\left\{ |u(t\,,0)-1| \leqslant |\sigma(1)| \theta t^{1/4}\ \ 
			\forall t \in [\varepsilon f(\varepsilon),\varepsilon]\right\} 
			= f(\varepsilon)^{\lambda(\theta)+\mathscr{o}(1)}\quad
			\text{as $\varepsilon\to0$},
		\]
		for every function $f:(0\,,1/{\rm e})\to(0\,,1/{\rm e})$ that vanishes at zero and satisfies
		$f(\varepsilon) \geqslant \varepsilon^{\ell+\mathscr{o}(1)}$, as $\varepsilon\to0^+$,
		for a fixed number $\ell=\ell(f)>0$.
	\item Let $K$ denote a nonrandom compact set in $\mathbb{R}$. Then,
		\[
			\mathrm{P}\{\fS(\theta)\cap K\neq\varnothing\}=
			\begin{cases}
				1 & \text{if $\lambda(\theta) < \frac12\dimh K$},\\
				0 & \text{if $\lambda(\theta) > \frac12\dimmm K$},
			\end{cases}
		\]
		where $\dimmm$ and $\dimh$ respectively the lower Minkowski (or box) dimension
		and the Hausdorff dimension (see Falconer \textnormal{\cite{Falconer}}); and
	\item  There exists a single $\mathrm{P}$-null set off which $\dimh \fS(\theta) = 1 - 2\lambda(\theta)$
		for every 
		\begin{equation}\label{theta_c}
			\theta\geqslant \theta_c := \lambda^{-1}(\tfrac12).
		\end{equation}
	\end{enumerate}
sketch:
A proof sketch appears only in the discussion of measurability:

\begin{remark}\label{rem:meas}
	As usual, the completeness condition for the underlying probability space
	can be made without loss in generality. It is used here in order to overcome 
	potential measurability issues. For instance, we establish Part (2) by proving 
	that $\{\omega\in\Omega:\ \fS(\theta)\cap K\neq\varnothing\}$
	is a subset of a $\P$-null set when $\lambda(\theta) > \frac12\dimmm K$,
	and contains a full-probability set when $\lambda(\theta) < \frac12\dimh K$.
	Likewise, Part (3) is proved by showing
	that the set $\{\omega\in\Omega:\ \dimh \fS(\theta)(\omega) \neq 1 - 
	2\lambda(\theta)\}$ lies in a $\P$-null set. In the sequel, we 
	might suppress discussions
	on measurability. In all such cases, measurability issues can be verified individually using this type of
	inclusion/exclusion idea.
\end{remark}

- The completeness assumption on $(\Omega,\mathscr{F},\mathrm{P})$ is used to handle “potential measurability issues.”

- In establishing the main theorem, Part~(2) is handled by proving that $\{\omega\in\Omega:\ \fS(\theta)\cap K\neq\varnothing\}$ is a subset of a $\mathrm{P}$-null set when $\lambda(\theta) > \frac12\dimmm K$, and contains a full-probability set when $\lambda(\theta) < \frac12\dimh K$.

- In establishing the main theorem, Part~(3) is proved by showing that the set $\{\omega\in\Omega:\ \dimh \fS(\theta)(\omega) \neq 1 - 2\lambda(\theta)\}$ lies in a $\mathrm{P}$-null set.

- They indicate that later they may “suppress discussions on measurability,” since such issues “can be verified individually using this type of inclusion/exclusion idea.”

No further proof outline for the analytic content (e.g., the construction/properties of $\lambda$ in Part~(1), or the probabilistic/fractal arguments beyond the above null/full-probability inclusions) is provided in the post-theorem introduction.

mcq.question:
Let $u=u(t,x)$ be the solution to the stochastic heat equation on $(0, ty)\times\mathbb R$
\[
\partial_t u(t,x)=\partial_x^2 u(t,x)+\sigma(u(t,x))\,\dot W(t,x),\qquad u(0,x)=1\ \text{for all }x\in\mathbb R,
\]
where $\sigma:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ is a nonrandom Lipschitz continuous function with $\sigma(1)\neq 0$, and $\dot W$ denotes space-time white noise. Assume the underlying probability space $(\Omega,\mathscr F,\mathrm P)$ is complete. Let
\[
\left\{|u(t,0)-1|\le |\sigma(1)|\,\theta\, t^{1/4}\ \text{for all } t\in[\varepsilon f(\varepsilon),\varepsilon]\right\}
\]
be the small-time event of interest, where $f:(0,1/e)\to(0,1/e)$ satisfies $f(\varepsilon)\to0$ as $\varepsilon\to0^+$ and
\[
f(\varepsilon)\ge \varepsilon^{\ell+o(1)}\quad\text{as }\varepsilon\to0^+
\]
for some fixed number $\ell=\ell(f)>0$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic probability of this event as $\varepsilon\to0^+$?

\]

theorem:
\label{Thm for E}
    If $E$ is uniformly weakly RC-positive over a compact K\"ahler manifold $X$, then $S^kE\otimes \det E$ is uniformly RC-positive for any $k\geq 0$, and $S^kE$ is uniformly RC-positive for $k$ large.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the text says that “instead of the fibration $p:P(E^*)\to X$, we will work on a more general fibration and prove a general theorem which contains the main theorem as a special case.” Concretely, one considers a proper holomorphic submersion $p:\mathcal{X}^{n+m}\to Y^m$ with $\mathcal{X}$ K\"ahler and $Y$ compact, a Hermitian line bundle $(L,h)$ on $\mathcal{X}$, and the direct-image bundle $V$ with fibers
\[
V_t=H^0(\mathcal{X}_t,\, L|_{\mathcal{X}_t}\otimes K_{\mathcal{X}_t}),
\]
endowed with the “naturally defined Hermitian metric”
\[
H(u,u):=\int_{\mathcal{X}_t} h(u,u).\tag{\ref{metric}}
\]
We first prove the following theorem.
\begin{theorem}\label{Thm 1}
    If the curvature $\Theta$ of $h$ is positive on every fiber, and for any point $t\in Y$, there exists a nonzero tangent vector $v\in T_tY$, such that $\Theta(\Tilde{v},\bar{\Tilde{v}})|_{(t,z)}>0$ for any lift $\Tilde{v}$ of $v$ to $T_{(t,z)}\mathcal{X}$, then the Hermitian bundle $(V,H)$ is uniformly RC-positive.
\end{theorem}
Under these hypotheses, one concludes that “the Hermitian bundle $(V,H)$ is uniformly RC-positive.”

Then the main theorem is obtained by applying the preceding theorem to $p:P(E^*)\to X$ (with $X$ K\"ahler so $P(E^*)$ is K\"ahler). In this specialization, one uses that
\[
K_{P(E^*)/X}\simeq O_{P(E^*)}(-r)\otimes p^*\det E.
\]
Choosing $L=O_{P(E^*)}(r+k)$ gives that the resulting direct image bundle $V$ is “$S^kE\otimes\det E$.” Alternatively, choosing $L=O_{P(E^*)}(k)\otimes K^{-1}_{P(E^*)/X}$ gives $V=S^kE$, and “the effect of [a metric on $K^{-1}_{P(E^*)/X}$] can be absorbed by taking $k$ large.”

Finally, the proof of the preceding theorem is described as “an adaptation of \cite[Section 3]{wu2025mean}” and attributed to Berndtsson (with references).

mcq.question: Let \(E\) be a holomorphic vector bundle of rank \(r\) over a compact K\"ahler manifold \(X\). Write \(S^kE\) for the \(k\)-th symmetric power of \(E\) and \(\det E\) for its determinant line bundle. Assume that \(E\) is uniformly weakly RC-positive, meaning the uniform version of weak RC-positivity: for some Hermitian metric on \(E\), the induced metric on the tautological line bundle \(O_{P(E^*)}(1)\) over the projectivized bundle \(P(E^*)\) has curvature positive on every fiber and at least \(r\) positive eigenvalues at every point. Here, a holomorphic vector bundle \(F\) is called uniformly RC-positive if for every point \(x\in X\) there exists a nonzero tangent vector \(u\in T_xX\) such that the Chern curvature satisfies \(R^F(u,\bar u,v,\bar v)>0\) for every nonzero fiber vector \(v\in F_x\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) and \(S^kE\) under these assumptions?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\) itself is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes (\det E)^{-1}\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\) itself is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^{k+r}E\) is uniformly RC-positive for every integer \(k\ge 0\).

theorem:
\label{vbint-}
Let $0<r\leq\infty$. 
Let $M$ be a domain in ${\bf C}^n$ with $\partial M\in C^2$. Assume that   the Levi form of $\partial M$ on $T^{(1,0)}\partial M$ has either $n-1$ positive or at least three negative eigenvalues at  $\zeta_0\in\partial M$.  
Let $E$ be a complex vector bundle of class $Lip$ $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1})$ over $\overline M$. Assume that a connection  $D\colon Lip(M,E)\to L_{(1)}^\infty(M,E)$  $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1}(M,E)\to \Lambda^{r}_{(1)}(M,E))$ satisfies $\Omega^{(0,2)}=0$ on $ M$ in the sense of distributions. Then $E|_U$ has a    holomorphic vector bundle structure, where $U$ is some open subset of $\overline M$ and $\zeta_0\in U$. Furthermore, the holomorphic structure is of class $C^{1/2}$ $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1/2})$ with respect to the original $Lip$ $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1})$ structure of $E|_U$.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, we reduce it to the following theorem.

\begin{thm}\label{vbint} 
Let $M$ be a domain in $\mathbb C^n$ with $\pd M\in C^2$. Assume that    the Levi-form of $M$ has
either $(n-1)$ positive or at least $3$ negative Levi eigenvalues at $\zeta_0\in\pd M$. Let $U$ be an open set in $\ov M$ and $\zeta_0\in U$.    Assume that $\om$ is a $k_0\times k_0$ matrix of $(0,1)$ forms on $U$ such that $\db\om=\om\wedge\om$ holds on $U\setminus\pd M$ in the sense of distributions. Assume that $\om$ is in $L^\infty(U)$ $($resp. $\Lambda^r(U)$ with $r>0)$. Then there is an invertible matrix $A$   of class $\Lambda^{1/2}$ $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1/2})$ on some neighborhood $U'$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\ov M$ such that 
$
\db A+A\om=0
$
in the sense of distributions on $U'\setminus\pd M$.
\end{thm}

The reduction requires a careful discussion of $L^\infty$ connections on Lipschitz bundles and of \textit{admissible} frame changes for which the transformation law (\ref{hatomom}) remains valid; for this we use the following proposition.

\begin{prop}\label{d2=0}
Let $D$ be a connection for a complex vector bundle $E$. Let $e=(e_1,\dots, e_{k_0})^t$ be a local frame field of $E$ over $U$. Assume that $De=\om \otimes e$ and $\om\in L^2_{loc}(U)$ and $d\om\in L^1_{loc}(U)$. If $A$ is admissible, then $\hat\om=(dA)A^{-1}+A\om A^{-1}$ is still in $L^2_{loc}(U)$ and  the curvature  forms    satisfy $A\Om A^{-1}=\hat\Om$. 
\end{prop}

“We now outline our approach. Equation (\ref{dbAA}) may be viewed as a nonlinear $\dbar$-equation, and the integrability condition (\ref{Om02=0}) is also nonlinear. We apply homotopy formulas to the study of (\ref{dbAA}).”

The proof is then completed as follows: Sections 3 and 4 “reformulate the local homotopy formulas … for shrinking domains,” Section 5 recalls and derives “estimates … on shrinking domains,” and finally we complete the proof of the main theorem by establishing the theorem above.

mcq.question: Let $0<r\leq \infty$. Let $M\subset \mathbf{C}^n$ be a domain with $\partial M\in C^2$, and let $\zeta_0\in \partial M$ be a boundary point such that the Levi form of $\partial M$ on $T^{(1,0)}\partial M$ has either $n-1$ positive eigenvalues or at least three negative eigenvalues at $\zeta_0$. Let $E\to \overline M$ be a complex vector bundle of class $Lip$ (respectively of class $\Lambda^{r+1}$). Suppose there is a connection $D\colon Lip(M,E)\to L_{(1)}^{\infty}(M,E)$ (respectively $D\colon \Lambda^{r+1}(M,E)\to \Lambda^r_{(1)}(M,E)$), where the curvature is $\Omega=D\circ D$ and $\Omega^{(0,2)}$ denotes its $(0,2)$-component, such that $\Omega^{(0,2)}=0$ on $M$ in the sense of distributions. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a holomorphic vector bundle structure on $E$ near $\zeta_0$ under these hypotheses?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists an open neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\overline M$ such that $E|_U$ admits a holomorphic vector bundle structure, and this holomorphic structure is of class $Lip$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1}$) with respect to the original $Lip$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1}$) structure of $E|_U$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists an open neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\overline M$ and a number $\alpha>0$ such that $E|_U$ admits a holomorphic vector bundle structure of class $C^{\alpha}$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+\alpha}$) with respect to the original $Lip$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1}$) structure of $E|_U$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists an open neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\overline M$ such that $E|_U$ admits a holomorphic vector bundle structure of class $C^{1/2}$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1/2}$) with respect to the original bundle structure, and in fact the same conclusion would still follow if the Levi form at $\zeta_0$ had merely at least two negative eigenvalues or at least one positive eigenvalue.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every open neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\overline M$, the restriction $E|_U$ admits a holomorphic vector bundle structure of class $C^{1/2}$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1/2}$) with respect to the original $Lip$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1}$) structure of $E|_U$.

theorem:
[Main theorem, informal]
Let $X$ be a local spectral expander whose links are $\beta$-coboundary expanders. For every group $\Gamma$ and every locally minimal cochain $f : X(k) \to \Gamma$, if $\left\|f\right\| \lesssim \beta^k/(k+2)!$ then $\left\|\delta f\right\| \ge c\left\|f\right\|$, where $c = \beta^k/(k!(k+1)^4)$.
sketch:
We found a bridge between these techniques that allows us to use the local-to-global averaging operators for any cochain which is not necessarily a cocycle. **Key Idea:** any general cochain either has a ``global'' structure (``it looks much like a cocycle'') or it has a ``local'' structure (``it is concentrated on links of some smaller dimension''). This enables applying the local-to-global averaging operators to general cochains.

Moreover, the method ``decouples between the coboundary and spectral expansion of the links'': in the ``global'' case expansion is deduced ``merely by spectral arguments'', while in the ``local'' case expansion is deduced ``merely by coboundary expansion of the links''.

Concretely, the proof proceeds by a case analysis of general cochains (Propositions 3.3 and 3.4): if the cochain is ``local'' one gets expansion from Proposition 3.3; if the cochain is ``global'' one gets expansion from Proposition 3.4. ``We prove our main theorem with these two propositions and then we turn to prove each proposition.''

mcq.question:
Let $X$ be a simplicial complex that is a local spectral expander, and suppose every link of $X$ is a $\beta$-coboundary expander. Fix a group $\Gamma$ and a locally minimal $k$-cochain $f\colon X(k)\to \Gamma$ (that is, a function on the set $X(k)$ of $k$-faces that is minimal on every link), where $\delta f$ denotes the coboundary of $f$ and $\|\cdot\|$ is the cochain norm. Assume that
$$\|f\|\lesssim \frac{\beta^k}{(k+2)!}.$$ 
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the coboundary norm $\|\delta f\|$ under these hypotheses?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
It satisfies the linear lower bound
$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$
with
$$c=\frac{\beta^k}{k!(k+2)^4},$$
that is,
$$\|\delta f\|\ge \frac{\beta^k}{k!(k+2)^4}\,\|f\|.$$

choices.label: C
choices.text:
It is nonzero whenever $f\neq 0$, i.e.
$$\|\delta f\|>0.$$

choices.label: D
choices.text:
It satisfies the linear lower bound
$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$
with
$$c=\frac{\beta^{k+1}}{k!(k+1)^4},$$
that is,
$$\|\delta f\|\ge \frac{\beta^{k+1}}{k!(k+1)^4}\,\|f\|.$$

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a constant
$$c=\frac{\beta^k}{k!(k+1)^4}$$
such that for every locally minimal $k$-cochain $f\colon X(k)\to \Gamma$,
$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$
without needing the assumption
$$\|f\|\lesssim \frac{\beta^k}{(k+2)!}.$$
