theorem:
\label{Hao_Kodaira_codim_1}\\cite[][Thm. 1.3]{Hao_Nowhere_Vanishing_Holomorphic_One-Forms_on_Varieties_of_Kodaira_Codimension_One}
    Let $X$ be a smooth projective variety of Kodaira dimension $\kappa(X)=\dim X-1$. If $X$ has a nowhere vanishing holomorphic 1-form, then for any minimal model $X^{min}$ of $X$ there exists a finite quasi-\'etale covering $X'\rightarrow X^{min}$ such that any $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization $X''$ of $X'$ is a product $B\times E$, where $B$ is a minimal model of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.,
sketch:
In the projective case, Hao’s argument is summarized as follows: take a good minimal model $X^{\min}$ and its Iitaka fibration $f\colon X^{\min}\to Y$. By Popa–Schnell, “Kodaira dimension and zeros of holomorphic 1-forms” (Thm. 2.1), the nowhere-vanishing $1$-form $\omega$ “cannot come from $Y$”. Hence “the general fiber of $f$ is not contracted by the Albanese morphism”, so it maps to “a translate of a fixed elliptic curve $E\subset \operatorname{Alb}(X)$”. Since $\operatorname{Alb}(X)$ is projective, one “dualize[s] the inclusion $E\subset \operatorname{Alb}(X)$ to get a morphism” $\varphi\colon X^{\min}\to \operatorname{Alb}(X)\to E$, and then “$B$ … as well as the splitting $X''\cong B\times E$ … are then constructed from a general fiber of $\varphi\colon X^{\min}\to E$.

For the K\"ahler generalization (Theorem~\ref{main_result}), two obstacles are noted: one cannot apply Popa–Schnell, and $\operatorname{Alb}(X)$ is not projective so “the morphism $\varphi$ does not exists in general.” To overcome this, the authors “invoke Lin’s construction of an algebraic approximation via tautological models and families” to produce a deformation $\pi\colon \mathcal{X}\to V$ of $X$ and a $G$-equivariant tautological family of elliptic fibrations $\Pi\colon \mathcal{T}\to Y\times V\to V$, with $\Pi^{-1}(0)/G\to Y/G$ bimeromorphic to the Iitaka fibration of $X$, and with $\mathcal{X}\to V$ having a dense set of projective fibers; moreover $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{T}/G$ are “bimeromorphic over $V$” and the elliptic fibrations $p_v\colon \Pi^{-1}(v)\to Y$ are “locally isomorphic over $Y$” (independent of $v$ on an open cover).

They then “show that a small deformation of $X$ also admits a holomorphic 1-form without zeros”, so there exists $v_0\in V$ with $\mathcal{X}_{v_0}$ projective and carrying such a form. Since the Iitaka fibration of $\mathcal{X}_{v_0}$ is birational to $p_{v_0}/G$ and the $p_v$ are locally isomorphic, they “conclude that the general fiber of the Iitaka fibration of any $\mathcal{X}_v$ is isomorphic to a fixed elliptic curve,” addressing the first obstacle. “To finish the argument,” they “show that the elliptic fibration $p_0\colon \Pi^{-1}(0)\to Y$ is locally trivial” (locally a product), then “carefully describe the $G$-action,” and “taking a carefully chosen subquotient, yields the desired elliptic fiber bundle $Z\to B$” with trivial monodromy.

mcq.question: Let $X$ be a smooth projective variety over $\mathbb{C}$ with Kodaira dimension $\kappa(X)=\dim X-1$, and suppose that $X$ admits a nowhere vanishing holomorphic $1$-form. For a minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$, a finite quasi-\'etale cover means a finite morphism $X'\to X^{\min}$ that is \'etale in codimension one, and a $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization of $X'$ means a small birational model $X''\to X'$ with $X''$ $\mathbb{Q}$-factorial. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the structure of a minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ under these hypotheses?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For any minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ there exists a finite quasi-\'etale covering $X'\to X^{\min}$ such that $X'$ itself is isomorphic to a product $B\times E$, where $B$ is a minimal model of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For any minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ there exists a finite quasi-\'etale covering $X'\to X^{\min}$ and a $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization $X''$ of $X'$ together with an isomorphism $X''\cong B\times E$, where $B$ is a projective variety of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For any minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ and any finite quasi-\'etale covering $X'\to X^{\min}$, every $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization $X''$ of $X'$ is isomorphic to a product $B\times E$, where $B$ is a minimal model of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For any minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ there exists a finite \emph{\'etale} covering $X'\to X^{\min}$ such that any $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization $X''$ of $X'$ is isomorphic to a product $B\times E$, where $B$ is a minimal model of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.

sketch:
Our proof uses the joint Sato--Tate equidistribution of any two non-CM, twist-inequivalent newforms (Wong), based on Barnet-Lamb et al.  Writing for primes $p$
\[
a_\varphi(p)=2p^{\frac{k-1}{2}}\cos(2\pi\theta_\varphi(p)),\qquad a_\psi(p)=2p^{\frac{\ell-1}{2}}\cos(2\pi\theta_\psi(p)),\qquad (\theta_\varphi(p),\theta_\psi(p))\in[0,\tfrac12]^2,
\]
Wong’s theorem gives equidistribution of $(\theta_\varphi(p),\theta_\psi(p))$ in $[0,\tfrac12]^2$ with respect to density $(4\sin(2\pi u)\sin(2\pi v))^2\,du\,dv$.  As noted, “it follows that $1$, $\theta_\varphi(p)$, and $\theta_\psi(p)$ are linearly independent over $\mathbb{Q}$ for all $p$ in a set of density $1$”, i.e.
\begin{lemma}\label{lem:linindep}
There is a set $S\subseteq\{p\text{ prime}:p\nmid M\}$ of natural density $1$
such that, for any $p\in S$ and any pair $i\ne j$, the numbers
$1$, $\theta_i(p)$, and $\theta_j(p)$ are linearly independent
over $\Q$.
\end{lemma}

Because “mutual independence of the angles $\theta_\varphi$ for more than two newforms is not known,” this “presents an obstacle to proving the main theorem using only information on $a_f(p)a_g(p)$ at primes $p$” (except that “we will however give such a proof when $f$ and $g$ have different weights or levels”).  Instead, the approach is to show that “for almost any tuple of distinct primes $p_1,\ldots,p_d$, where $d=\dim S_k^{\rm new}(\Gamma_0(M))$, the non-zero $f\in S_k^{\rm new}(\Gamma_0(M))$ with real coefficients are determined modulo scalars by the signs of $a_f(n)$ for $n\in\{p_1^{k_1}\cdots p_d^{k_d}:k_1,\ldots,k_d\ge0\}$,” and for the precise statement we use the following proposition.
\begin{proposition}\label{prop:dense}
Let $p_1,\ldots,p_d\in S$ be distinct primes. Then
\[
\bigl\{
\bigl[a_1\bigl(p_1^{k_1}\cdots p_d^{k_d}\bigr):\cdots:a_d\bigl(p_1^{k_1}\cdots p_d^{k_d}\bigr)\bigl]\in\RP^{d-1}:
k_1,\ldots,k_d\ge0
\bigl\}
\]
is dense in $\RP^{d-1}$.
\end{proposition}

mcq.question: Let $M,N\in\mathbb{N}$ be such that $\operatorname{lcm}(M,N)$ is divisible by neither $2^4$ nor the square of any odd prime. Let $f\in S_k^{\rm new}(\Gamma_0(M))$ and $g\in S_\ell^{\rm new}(\Gamma_0(N))$ be nonzero new cusp forms with real Fourier coefficients $a_f(n)$ and $a_g(n)$, where $S_w^{\rm new}(\Gamma_0(L))$ denotes the space of weight-$w$ new cusp forms on $\Gamma_0(L)$. Which statement is equivalent to the condition that $a_f(n)a_g(n)\ge 0$ for all sufficiently large $n\in\mathbb{N}$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is equivalent to requiring that $(M,k)=(N,\ell)$ and $f/g$ is a nonzero real constant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It implies that $(M,k)=(N,\ell)$ and $f/g$ is a constant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is equivalent to requiring that $a_f(p)a_g(p)\ge 0$ for all sufficiently large primes $p$; in that case one must have $(M,k)=(N,\ell)$ and $f/g$ a positive constant.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is equivalent to requiring that $(M,k)=(N,\ell)$ and there exists a constant $c>0$ such that $|a_f(n)|=c\,|a_g(n)|$ for all sufficiently large $n\in\mathbb{N}$.

mcq.question:
Let \(X\) be a compact arithmetic congruence complex hyperbolic surface, and let \(\psi\) be an \(L^2\)-normalized Hecke--Maass form on \(X\) with spectral parameter \(\lambda>0\). Let \(\Pi\) denote the space of unit-length geodesic segments in \(X\), and for \(\gamma\in\Pi\) let \(\mathcal T_{\lambda^{-1/2}}(\gamma)\) be the \(\lambda^{-1/2}\)-neighborhood of \(\gamma)\). If the Kakeya--Nikodym norm is defined by
\[
\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN}:=\sup_{\gamma\in\Pi}\|\psi|_{\mathcal T_{\lambda^{-1/2}}(\gamma)}\|_2,
\]
what is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN}\) for every \(\epsilon>0\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every \(\epsilon>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/28+\epsilon}\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\) and \(\epsilon\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists an absolute constant \(A>0\) such that \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/28}(\log \lambda)^A\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists some \(\delta>0\) such that \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-\delta}\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every \(\epsilon>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/24+\epsilon}\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\) and \(\epsilon\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a constant \(C_X>0\) such that for every \(\epsilon>0\) and every \(\lambda>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \le C_X\lambda^{-1/28+\epsilon}\).

Let $X$ be a tropical variety in $\mathbb R^n$, and let $|X|$ denote its support. Define
$$\mathbf E(|X|):=\{(f,g)\in \mathbb T[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\mid f|_{|X|}=g|_{|X|}\},$$
the congruence on the tropical polynomial semiring consisting of pairs of tropical polynomials that agree on $|X|$. Which statement holds for every such tropical variety $X$?

sketch:
For the sufficient direction (To prove the main theorem, ...), the text compares to the elliptic strategy of Kilpeläinen–Zhong, *Removability of sets for quasilinear elliptic equations* and then states the authors’ parabolic proof route.

- Elliptic benchmark strategy (as a guide): take a solution $u$ in $\Omega\setminus E$, solve an obstacle problem in all of $\Omega$ with obstacle $u$ to get $v$; control the oscillation of $v$ from that of $u$ near the contact set via Harnack; use Caccioppoli to bound the Riesz measure $\mu$ of $v$; the size assumption on $E$ gives $\mu(E)=0$, while $\mu(\Omega\setminus E)=0$ since $u$ solves there; conclude $\mu\equiv 0$ so $v$ solves the PDE, and finally show $v=u$ so $u$ solves in all of $\Omega$.

- Parabolic difficulty: “using the Harnack inequality is more delicate due to the presence of the time lag”; for general operators “the regularity of solutions is not sufficient to perform the reduction to $p=2$,” and for $p>2$ “the time lag cannot be controlled by a constant scaling…,” so proving an oscillation estimate for $v$ “seems to be a difficult problem.”

- Claimed proof strategy used for the main theorem (Next we prove...): instead of an oscillation estimate for $v$, they “directly show the decay of the Riesz measure associated with the solution $v$ of the obstacle problem,” using “a Caccioppoli inequality, truncation arguments, and elementary properties of the obstacle problem.” Concretely: (i) “for any supersolution $w$… estimate the Riesz measure associated with $w$ in terms of the oscillation of $w$”; (ii) “by truncating $v$, the oscillation of the truncated function is controlled by the oscillation of the obstacle”; (iii) truncation “only increases the associated Riesz measure,” so “the Riesz measure of $v$ can be estimated by the oscillation of the obstacle.”

For the converse/necessity direction, we first prove the following theorem.
\begin{theorem}
\label{thm: necessity of Hausdorff zero}
Let $\cA$ satisfy \eqref{as:growth} and \eqref{as:monotonicity-stronger}, let $\kappa \in (0, 1]$ be such that~\eqref{eq: kappa hlder cont of sols} holds, and let $0<\alpha<\kappa$. Let $E\subset\Omega_T$ be a closed set with $\mathcal{H}^{n+\alpha}_{(\alpha)}(E)>0$. Then, there exists a function $u$ which is locally $\alpha$-Hlder continuous in $\Omega_T$ with respect to $d_\alpha$, such that $u$ is a solution of \eqref{eq_main} in $\Omega_T\backslash E$, but not in $\Omega_T$.  
\end{theorem}
The text gives the following sketch (We prove later...): construct “a nonzero measure $\mu$ supported on $E$” and a function $u$ solving the measure-data problem
\[
\partial_t u-\mathcal{A}(x,t,\nabla u)=\mu
\]
weakly; build $\mu$ via “a parabolic Frostman Lemma,” which also provides “a suitable decay”; this decay “ensures the H\"older continuity of the solution” by “a suitable comparison estimate for solutions to measure data problems and a delicate intrinsic iteration technique.”

mcq.question:
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb R^n\) be open, let \(0<T<\infty\), and write \(\Omega_T=\Omega\times(0,T)\). Let \(E\subset \Omega_T\) be closed, and let \(\mathcal A(x,t,\xi)\) satisfy for some constants \(\nu,L>0\) and \(p>1\)
\[
\mathcal A(x,t,\xi)\cdot \xi\ge \nu |\xi|^p,
\qquad |
\mathcal A(x,t,\xi)|\le L|\xi|^{p-1},
\]
and the strict monotonicity condition
\[
\bigl(\mathcal A(x,t,\xi)-\mathcal A(x,t,\eta)\bigr)\cdot(\xi-\eta)>0
\quad\text{for all }\xi\ne\eta\text{ in }\mathbb R^n.
\]
Assume \(u\in C(\Omega_T)\) is a weak solution of
\[
\partial_t u-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(x,t,\nabla u)=0
\]
in \(\Omega_T\setminus E\), and that for some \(M>0\) and \(\alpha>0\),
\[
|u(z)-u(z_0)|\le M\, d_{\alpha}(z,z_0)^{\alpha}
\]
for every \(z_0\in E\) and \(z\in \Omega_T\), where for \(z=(x,t)\) and \(z_0=(y,s)\)
\[
d_{\alpha}(z,z_0)=\max\Big\{|x-y|,\ |t-s|^{1/(p+\alpha(2-p))}\Big\},
\]
and \(\mathcal H^{\sigma}_{(\alpha)}\) is the Hausdorff measure associated with coverings by cylinders
\[
Q_{r,r^{\alpha}}(x_0,t_0)=B(x_0,r)\times \bigl(t_0-r^{\,p+\alpha(2-p)},\, t_0+r^{\,p+\alpha(2-p)}\bigr),
\]
namely
\[
\mathcal H^{\sigma}_{(\alpha)}(F)=\lim_{\delta\downarrow 0}\inf\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} r_i^{\sigma}:\ F\subset \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} Q_{r_i,r_i^{\alpha}}(z_i),\ 0<r_i<\delta\right\}.
\]
If \(\mathcal H^{n+\alpha}_{(\alpha)}(E)=0\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(u\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
Then \(u\) is a weak solution of
\[
\partial_t u-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(x,t,\nabla u)=0
\]
in \(\Omega_T\); moreover, the same conclusion would still hold if the assumption \(\mathcal H^{n+\alpha}_{(\alpha)}(E)=0\) were weakened to \(\mathcal H^{n+\alpha}_{(\alpha)}(E)<\infty\).

choices.label: C
choices.text:
Then \(u\) admits a weak-solution extension across \(E\): there exists \(\widetilde u\in C(\Omega_T)\) such that \(\widetilde u=u\) in \(\Omega_T\setminus E\) and
\[
\partial_t \widetilde u-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(x,t,\nabla \widetilde u)=0
\]
weakly in \(\Omega_T\).

choices.label: D
choices.text:
Then \(u\) is a weak solution of
\[
\partial_t u-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(x,t,\nabla u)=0
\]
in \(\Omega_T\) whenever \(\mathcal H^{n+\alpha}_{(\beta)}(E)=0\) for some \(\beta\in(0,1]\), even if \(\beta\neq \alpha\).

choices.label: E
choices.text:
Then \(u\) is a weak solution of
\[
\partial_t u-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(x,t,\nabla u)=0
\]
in \(\Omega_T\) even if the Hölder condition is replaced by the pointwise family
\[
|u(z)-u(z_0)|\le M_{z_0}\, d_{\alpha}(z,z_0)^{\alpha}
\]
for every \(z_0\in E\) and \(z\in \Omega_T\), with constants \(M_{z_0}>0\) allowed to depend on \(z_0\) and not assumed uniformly bounded on \(E\).

sketch:
To prove the above power counting formulas, the authors “introduce several techniques in the asymptotic analysis of divided differences, and prove upper and lower bounds for them.” A “pivotal concept throughout the proof is the weighted divided difference
$$f'\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}:=(-1)^n x_1\cdots x_n f'[x_1,\ldots,x_n],$$
whose asymptotic behavior is “more easily understood, because sending any of its variables to infinity yields another weighted divided difference.”

A “key result” used in the proof is that “weighted divided differences are positive on large enough subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ for the functions $f$ we are considering,” which generalizes Hunter’s positivity theorem. Moreover, for functions “bounded above or below by $x^{-p}$, with similar bounds on their derivatives,” they show that “the asymptotic behavior of the weighed divided differences away from the origin is determined by the variable with the smallest modulus,” and this is stated to be “crucial in the proof of our main theorem.”

Finally, “our main theorem is proved by combining this positivity and the mentioned upper and lower bounds with an interesting graph-theoretical argument.”,

mcq.question: In the spectral action matrix model, let $N\in\mathbb{N}$, let $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_N$ be spectral parameters, and for any listed arguments let $f'[\mu_0,\dots,\mu_m]$ denote the divided difference of $f'$, defined inductively by $f'[x]:=f'(x)$ and $f'[x,y]:=\frac{f'(x)-f'(y)}{x-y}$ (and similarly for higher orders). For fixed external labels $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, consider the two ribbon-graph amplitudes with one vertex carrying external labels $i$ and $j$: (1) the amplitude with a single attached loop, and (2) the amplitude with two attached loops. Which statement holds for every such pair $(i,j)$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_l]}.
\]
Thus each added loop contributes one second-order divided-difference factor, both referenced to the external label $j$.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude is given by
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
while the two-loop amplitude is a double sum over $k,l\in\{1,\dots,N\}$ whose numerator is
\[
f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i],
\]
and whose denominator is a product of two second-order divided differences.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_l]f'[\lambda_k,\lambda_l]}.
\]
In other words, the two denominator factors are attached to the pairs $(j,l)$ and $(k,l)$.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_l,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]f'[\lambda_k,\lambda_l]}.
\]
So the denominator has the same two factors as in the standard two-loop pattern, but the higher divided difference repeats the internal label $l$ rather than $k$.

theorem: \label{t:mainthm1} Fix $(M^{2n}, J)$ an OT manifold and $g_0$ a pluriclosed metric on $M$. The solution to pluriclosed flow with initial data $g_0$ exists on $[0, \infty)$.,
sketch: The proof of To prove the main theorem, is described only at a high level: it “exploits [a] natural class of background metrics arising from the homogeneous structure on OT-manifolds.” In the case of Inoue surfaces, “these are known as Tricerri metrics.”

mcq.question: Let \((M^{2n},J)\) be an OT manifold (that is, an Oeljeklaus--Toma complex manifold, with complex structure \(J\)), and let \(g_0\) be a pluriclosed metric on \(M\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the pluriclosed flow with initial metric \(g_0\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The all-time existence conclusion is guaranteed only when the initial metric \(g_0\) belongs to the OT-homogeneous background class (for instance, a Tricerri-type metric), and not for an arbitrary pluriclosed metric.

choices.label: C
choices.text: In particular, the flow can be continued through every finite time: for each \(T>0\), the solution with initial data \(g_0\) exists on \([0,T]\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: What is guaranteed is a uniform positive lifespan depending only on the OT manifold \((M,J)\), so the solution exists on some interval \([0,T]\) independent of \(g_0\), but no general conclusion that it exists for all \(t\ge 0\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: The theorem guarantees more than existence: after passing to the normalized pluriclosed flow, every solution starting from a pluriclosed \(g_0\) converges in the Gromov--Hausdorff sense to the canonical torus associated to the OT data.
